title: Acid-base balance
body_html:
Acidosis and alkalosis
The acid or alkali status of a solution is determined by the pH. In chemistry a neutral pH is 7, solutions with a pH less than 7 are acids whilst those with a pH above 7 are alkali.

In humans the normal pH range is 7.35-7.45. Acidosis in humans refers to a pH below 7.35 whilst alkalosis refers to a pH above 7.45

It is useful to have an idea of what pH is when considering acid/base problems. pH is determined by hydrogen ion [H+] concentration and specifically by the equation:

pH = -log10 [H+]

pH uses an inverse logarithmic scale so when hydrogen ion concentration goes up the pH value goes down. As its logarithmic when the pH value changes by 1 the hydrogen ion concentration has changed by a factor of 10.

Increasing hydrogen concentration = more acidic = lower pH

Metabolism produces approximately 60 mmol of H+ ions each day and the majority is combined with bicarbonate (HCO3-) to form CO2 and water which is mostly excreted through the lungs.

The bicarbonate used up for handling these hydrogen ions is replaced by the kidneys.

Controlling pH
The body tries to maintain a a pH between 7.35 and 7.45. pH homeostasis is achieved via the following:
  • 1. Respiratory control: adjusting ventilation to control CO2 levels
  • 2. Metabolic buffers: several buffers help control pH levels by 'mopping up' excess acid or alkali. This includes the bicarbonate buffer system, heamaglobin system (chloride shift), plasma protein buffer system and phosphate buffer system.
  • 3. Renal pH control: The kidneys adjust excretion of H+ ions into the urine and secretion of bicarbonate into the blood by the renal tubular cells of the kidneys.
  • 4. Potassium exchange: The body can exchange potassium (K+) and hydrogen (H+) ions between the intracellular and extracellular spaces. If the blood becomes too acidic excess H+ can be pumped from the blood into the cells with potassium moving in the opposite direction. Vice versa If the blood is too basic (low H+ / alkali) the body can pump hydrogen ions out of cells into the blood with potassium moving in the opposite direction in exchange.

Buffers
Buffers are weak acids (HA) or weak bases (A-) which are able to donate or accept H+ ions. Buffers are often referred to as buffer pairs as they may exist in two forms depending on whether they have accepted or donated a H+ ion e.g. H2CO3 and HCO3-.

The ratio between the pair is determined by hydrogen ion concentration [H+] and the dissociation constant (K) for that buffer pair. This relationship is described by the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:

pH = pK + log([A»]/[HA])

Changes in [A-] or [HA] will have a predictable effect on pH as shown in the table below:

Changeeffect on pH
↑[A-]↑
↓[A-]↓
↑[HA]↓
↓[HA]↑

Buffers work best when pH is close to their pK value.

Buffer systems
The bicarbonate buffer system is the most important in the human body. It can be represented by the equation below. The reversible reaction where carbonic acid is converted to carbon dioxide and water is catalysed by the enzyme carbonic anhydrase.

H+ + HCO3- ‡Œ H2CO3 ‡Œ CO2 + H2O

The bicarbonate buffer system has a pK of 6.1 which is not particularly close to plasma pH of around 7.4. It remains effective as CO2 & HCO3- are precisely controlled by the lungs and kidney respectively. This means the HCO3- / H2CO3 ratio remains constant and as a result so does pH.

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation can be applied to the bicarbonate buffer system as below:
pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3»]/[H2CO3])

because [H2CO3] is directly proportional to [CO2] and [CO2] = pCO2 x s (where s is solubility) the equation can be derived as below:

pH = 6.1 + log( [HCO3»] / pCO2 x s)


Haemaglobin provides around 20% of buffering capacity in the blood. Within cells proteins and phosphate are the intracellular buffers. In the urine ammonia and phosphate allow for the excretion of large quantities of H+
